name: middleware
context: Run code before Next.js requests complete — authentication, redirects, rewrites, headers, and geo-routing with middleware.ts
info: Next.js Middleware runs before a request is completed, allowing you to modify the response by rewriting, redirecting, modifying headers, or setting cookies. Middleware is defined in a single `middleware.ts` file at the project root (or `src/` root). It runs on every request by default but can be scoped with a `matcher` config. Middleware uses the Edge Runtime — a lightweight subset of Node.js APIs based on Web Standards (Request, Response, fetch, crypto). Common use cases include authentication checks, locale detection, A/B testing, bot protection, feature flags, and request logging. Middleware executes before cached content and route matching, making it the earliest interception point in the request lifecycle.

sections.title: Setup and matcher
sections.body: Place `middleware.ts` at the project root (next to `app/` or `pages/`) or inside `src/`. Only one middleware file per project. Use `export const config = { matcher: [...] }` to scope middleware to specific paths. Without a matcher, middleware runs on every request including `_next/static` and `_next/image` — always use a matcher in production. Matchers support named params (`:path*`) and regex patterns.

sections.title: Authentication patterns
sections.body: Check for session tokens in `request.cookies`. Redirect unauthenticated users to a login page with `NextResponse.redirect()`. Pass the original URL as a `callbackUrl` query parameter for post-login redirect. For JWT validation, use edge-compatible libraries (jose, not jsonwebtoken). For NextAuth.js, use `withAuth` middleware wrapper. Always protect both page routes and API routes.

sections.title: Redirects and rewrites
sections.body: `NextResponse.redirect(url)` sends a 307 redirect (or specify 301/308). `NextResponse.rewrite(url)` serves content from a different URL without changing the browser URL — useful for A/B testing, feature flags, and proxying external APIs. Both construct URLs with `new URL(path, request.url)` to preserve the origin. Rewrites can point to external domains.

sections.title: Edge Runtime limitations
sections.body: Middleware runs on the Edge Runtime, which has a limited API surface compared to Node.js. Available: `fetch`, `Request`, `Response`, `Headers`, `URL`, `crypto`, `TextEncoder/TextDecoder`, `setTimeout`, `setInterval`. Not available: `fs`, `path`, `child_process`, `net`, native Node.js modules. Use edge-compatible npm packages. Max execution time varies by platform (typically 30s on Vercel).
